name: paracetamol
when_to_use: Used to reduce fever and relieve mild to moderate pain such as headache, body pain, toothache, cold-related discomfort, and post-vaccination fever. It is commonly used for short-term symptom relief when pain or fever is present.
how_to_use: Adults can take 500–650 mg every 6 hours if required. It should be taken with a full glass of water, preferably after food to reduce stomach irritation. Do not exceed 4000 mg per day. Always follow doctor guidance for children dosage.
side_effects: Generally safe when taken correctly. Overdose may cause serious liver damage. Some people may experience nausea, allergic skin rash, or mild stomach discomfort.
age_limit: Can be used in children above 6 years with proper dose adjustment. Safe for adults and elderly under medical supervision.

name: ibuprofen
when_to_use: Used to relieve pain and inflammation caused by muscle strain, joint pain, menstrual cramps, arthritis, dental pain, and minor injuries such as sprains. Also helps in reducing mild fever.
how_to_use: Recommended dose is 200–400 mg every 6–8 hours after meals. Should be taken with water and not on an empty stomach. Long-term use should only be under medical advice.
side_effects: May cause stomach irritation, heartburn, nausea, dizziness, and in rare cases stomach ulcers or kidney problems if used excessively.
age_limit: Suitable for individuals above 12 years. Elderly patients or those with kidney/heart issues should consult a doctor before use.

name: cetirizine
when_to_use: Used for treating allergy symptoms including sneezing, runny nose, watery eyes, itching, and skin rashes caused by dust, pollen, or seasonal allergies.
how_to_use: Take 10 mg once daily, usually in the evening. It can be taken with or without food. Avoid driving if drowsiness occurs.
side_effects: Common side effects include drowsiness, dry mouth, mild headache, or fatigue. Usually mild and temporary.
age_limit: Recommended for children above 6 years and adults. Lower doses are used for younger children.

name: vitaminD3
when_to_use: Prescribed for Vitamin D deficiency, weak bones, low immunity, osteoporosis prevention, and to improve calcium absorption in the body.
how_to_use: Typically 1000–2000 IU daily after meals. In severe deficiency, higher weekly or monthly doses may be prescribed by a doctor.
side_effects: Excessive intake may cause nausea, vomiting, weakness, or high calcium levels in blood. Overuse should be avoided.
age_limit: Can be used in all age groups with appropriate dosage as per doctor recommendation.

name: iron
when_to_use: Used to treat iron deficiency anemia, low hemoglobin levels, weakness, fatigue, and during pregnancy when iron requirement increases.
how_to_use: Usually taken once daily on an empty stomach for better absorption. Can be taken with vitamin C (like orange juice). Avoid milk or tea immediately after taking.
side_effects: May cause constipation, stomach upset, nausea, and dark-colored stools. These are common and usually manageable.
age_limit: Suitable for children above 5 years and adults under medical supervision.

name: calcium carbonate
when_to_use: Used to strengthen bones and teeth, prevent osteoporosis, treat calcium deficiency, especially in elderly individuals and post-menopausal women.
how_to_use: Take 500–1000 mg daily after meals with water. Often combined with Vitamin D for better absorption and effectiveness.
side_effects: May cause constipation, bloating, or mild abdominal discomfort. Excess use may increase risk of kidney stones.
age_limit: Mainly recommended for adults and elderly. Pediatric dosage requires medical advice.

name: aspirin
when_to_use: Used to prevent heart attack and stroke in patients who are at high cardiovascular risk. It works by reducing blood clot formation.
how_to_use: Take one 75 mg tablet daily after food with water. Do not crush or chew unless advised. Should be taken regularly only under doctor supervision.
side_effects: Can cause stomach irritation, gastric bleeding, bruising, or increased bleeding tendency. Not safe for people with bleeding disorders.
age_limit: Not recommended for children. Generally prescribed for adults above 18 years only.

name: pantoprazole
when_to_use: Used to treat acidity, acid reflux (GERD), stomach ulcers, and heartburn by reducing stomach acid production.
how_to_use: Take one tablet 30 minutes before breakfast. Swallow whole without crushing. Usually prescribed for short-term use unless otherwise advised.
side_effects: May cause headache, diarrhea, abdominal pain. Long-term use may reduce Vitamin B12 levels.
age_limit: Recommended for individuals above 12 years.

name: multivitamin tablet
when_to_use: Used to support overall health, improve immunity, prevent nutritional deficiencies, and provide essential vitamins and minerals lacking in daily diet.
how_to_use: Take one tablet daily after breakfast with water. Do not exceed recommended dose unless advised by a doctor.
side_effects: Generally safe. Rarely may cause mild nausea, stomach upset, or allergic reaction.
age_limit: Suitable for individuals above 12 years. Separate pediatric formulations available for children.

name: metformin
when_to_use: Prescribed for management of Type 2 Diabetes to control blood sugar levels and improve insulin sensitivity in the body.
how_to_use: Usually 500 mg once or twice daily with meals to reduce stomach upset. Dose may gradually increase depending on blood sugar levels.
side_effects: Common side effects include diarrhea, nausea, bloating, and metallic taste. Rarely may cause lactic acidosis in high-risk patients.
age_limit: Generally prescribed for adults above 18 years under medical supervision.

name: amoxicillin
when_to_use: Used to treat bacterial infections such as throat infections, ear infections, urinary tract infections, and respiratory infections. It works by stopping the growth of bacteria.
how_to_use: Usually taken 250–500 mg every 8 hours as prescribed by a doctor. Should be taken with or without food, but at the same time each day. Complete the full course even if symptoms improve.
side_effects: Nausea, diarrhea, skin rash, allergic reactions. Severe allergy (rare) may cause breathing difficulty.
age_limit: Suitable for children and adults with doctor prescription. Dose varies by age and weight.

name: azithromycin
when_to_use: Prescribed for bacterial infections including chest infections, sinusitis, skin infections, and some sexually transmitted infections.
how_to_use: Typically taken once daily for 3–5 days as directed. Can be taken with or without food. Avoid antacids within 2 hours of taking the tablet.
side_effects: Stomach upset, diarrhea, nausea, abdominal pain. Rarely heart rhythm changes.
age_limit: Used in children and adults with prescription. Dosage adjusted based on age and weight.

name: levocetirizine
when_to_use: Used to relieve allergy symptoms such as sneezing, runny nose, watery eyes, and itching caused by seasonal allergies or dust exposure.
how_to_use: Usually 5 mg once daily in the evening. Can be taken with or without food. Avoid alcohol while using.
side_effects: Drowsiness, dry mouth, fatigue, mild headache.
age_limit: Recommended for individuals above 6 years. Lower doses for younger children as prescribed.

name: ranitidine
when_to_use: Used to reduce stomach acid in cases of acidity, heartburn, and gastric ulcers.
how_to_use: Usually taken once or twice daily as directed by a doctor. Can be taken before meals or at bedtime.
side_effects: Headache, constipation, diarrhea, rarely confusion in elderly.
age_limit: Suitable for adults and children above 12 years under supervision.

name: dicycloverine
when_to_use: Used to relieve stomach cramps, abdominal pain, and irritable bowel syndrome (IBS).
how_to_use: Typically taken 10–20 mg up to four times daily before meals. Follow doctor’s dosage instructions carefully.
side_effects: Dry mouth, dizziness, blurred vision, nausea.
age_limit: Generally prescribed for adults above 18 years.

name: loperamide
when_to_use: Used to control diarrhea by slowing down bowel movement and reducing stool frequency.
how_to_use: Initial dose usually 4 mg, followed by 2 mg after each loose stool. Do not exceed recommended daily limit.
side_effects: Constipation, stomach cramps, dizziness. Overuse may cause serious heart problems.
age_limit: Recommended for individuals above 6 years. Avoid in children under 6.

name: ondansetron
when_to_use: Used to prevent nausea and vomiting caused by food poisoning, stomach infection, or certain treatments.
how_to_use: Usually taken 4–8 mg as prescribed. Can be taken with or without food.
side_effects: Headache, constipation, dizziness. Rarely may affect heart rhythm.
age_limit: Suitable for children and adults with prescription. Dose adjusted by age.

name: atorvastatin
when_to_use: Used to lower cholesterol levels and reduce risk of heart disease and stroke.
how_to_use: Taken once daily, usually in the evening, with or without food. Long-term use requires regular medical check-ups.
side_effects: Muscle pain, weakness, mild digestive problems. Rarely liver issues.
age_limit: Prescribed for adults above 18 years.

name: losartan
when_to_use: Used to treat high blood pressure and protect kidneys in diabetic patients.
how_to_use: Taken once daily at the same time each day, with or without food. Regular blood pressure monitoring is required.
side_effects: Dizziness, low blood pressure, high potassium levels.
age_limit: Generally prescribed for adults above 18 years.

name: salbutamol tablet
when_to_use: Used to relieve breathing problems such as asthma, bronchitis, and airway obstruction by relaxing airway muscles.
how_to_use: Usually taken 2–4 mg up to three times daily as directed by a doctor.
side_effects: Tremors, fast heartbeat, nervousness, headache.
age_limit: Can be used in children and adults under prescription with proper dose adjustment.

name: amlodipine
when_to_use: Used to treat high blood pressure (hypertension) and chest pain (angina). It helps relax blood vessels and improve blood flow.
how_to_use: Usually taken once daily with or without food, at the same time each day. Dose depends on medical condition.
side_effects: Swelling of ankles, dizziness, flushing, fatigue.
age_limit: Prescribed for adults above 18 years.

name: clopidogrel
when_to_use: Used to prevent blood clots in patients at risk of heart attack or stroke.
how_to_use: Taken once daily with water, with or without food. Should be taken regularly as prescribed.
side_effects: Increased bleeding, bruising, stomach upset.
age_limit: Adults above 18 years under prescription.

name: montelukast
when_to_use: Used to prevent asthma symptoms and relieve allergy-related breathing problems.
how_to_use: Usually taken once daily in the evening, with or without food.
side_effects: Headache, abdominal pain, mood changes (rare).
age_limit: Suitable for children above 6 years and adults.

name: domperidone
when_to_use: Used to relieve nausea, vomiting, and bloating by improving stomach movement.
how_to_use: Taken 15–30 minutes before meals, usually 1–3 times daily.
side_effects: Dry mouth, dizziness, irregular heartbeat (rare).
age_limit: Generally for individuals above 12 years.

name: prednisolone
when_to_use: Used to reduce inflammation in conditions like asthma, arthritis, skin disorders, and allergies.
how_to_use: Dose varies widely; taken after food to reduce stomach irritation. Must not be stopped suddenly.
side_effects: Weight gain, mood changes, high blood sugar, weakened immunity.
age_limit: Prescribed for children and adults under strict medical supervision.

name: hydrochlorothiazide
when_to_use: Used to treat high blood pressure and fluid retention (edema).
how_to_use: Taken once daily in the morning to avoid nighttime urination.
side_effects: Frequent urination, dizziness, low potassium levels.
age_limit: Adults above 18 years.

name: gabapentin
when_to_use: Used to treat nerve pain and seizures.
how_to_use: Usually started at a low dose and gradually increased. Taken with or without food.
side_effects: Drowsiness, dizziness, coordination problems.
age_limit: Adults and children above 6 years under prescription.

name: tramadol
when_to_use: Used to relieve moderate to severe pain after injury or surgery.
how_to_use: Taken every 4–6 hours as prescribed. Should not exceed recommended dosage.
side_effects: Nausea, dizziness, constipation, risk of dependence.
age_limit: Adults above 18 years only.

name: ciprofloxacin
when_to_use: Used to treat bacterial infections such as urinary, respiratory, and gastrointestinal infections.
how_to_use: Usually taken twice daily with plenty of water. Avoid dairy products at the same time.
side_effects: Nausea, diarrhea, tendon pain (rare but serious).
age_limit: Adults above 18 years; limited use in children.

name: fluconazole
when_to_use: Used to treat fungal infections like oral thrush and vaginal infections.
how_to_use: Often taken as a single dose or daily as prescribed. Can be taken with or without food.
side_effects: Headache, stomach pain, nausea, liver issues (rare).
age_limit: Suitable for adults and children with prescription.

name: warfarin
when_to_use: Used to prevent and treat blood clots in veins and heart conditions.
how_to_use: Taken once daily at the same time. Requires regular blood tests (INR monitoring).
side_effects: Bleeding risk, bruising, nausea.
age_limit: Adults above 18 years under strict supervision.

name: insulin tablet
when_to_use: Used to lower blood sugar levels in Type 2 Diabetes.
how_to_use: Usually taken once daily before breakfast or as directed.
side_effects: Low blood sugar, weight gain, dizziness.
age_limit: Adults above 18 years.

name: alprazolam
when_to_use: Used to treat anxiety and panic disorders.
how_to_use: Taken 0.25–0.5 mg as prescribed. Should not be stopped suddenly.
side_effects: Drowsiness, dependency risk, confusion.
age_limit: Adults above 18 years only.

name: escitalopram
when_to_use: Used to treat depression and anxiety disorders.
how_to_use: Taken once daily, usually in the morning or evening.
side_effects: Nausea, sleep disturbance, sexual dysfunction.
age_limit: Adults above 18 years.

name: metoprolol
when_to_use: Used to treat high blood pressure, chest pain, and heart rhythm disorders.
how_to_use: Taken once or twice daily with meals.
side_effects: Slow heart rate, fatigue, dizziness.
age_limit: Adults above 18 years.

name: loratadine
when_to_use: Used for allergy symptoms such as sneezing and itchy eyes.
how_to_use: 10 mg once daily, with or without food.
side_effects: Headache, dry mouth, mild drowsiness.
age_limit: Above 6 years.

name: tamsulosin
when_to_use: Used to treat urinary problems due to enlarged prostate in men.
how_to_use: Taken once daily after the same meal each day.
side_effects: Dizziness, low blood pressure, ejaculation issues.
age_limit: Adult men only (usually above 40 years).

name: allopurinol
when_to_use: Used to prevent gout attacks by reducing uric acid levels.
how_to_use: Taken once daily after meals with plenty of water.
side_effects: Skin rash, nausea, rare severe allergic reaction.
age_limit: Adults above 18 years.

name: baclofen
when_to_use: Used to relieve muscle stiffness and spasms.
how_to_use: Usually taken 2–3 times daily as prescribed. Dose gradually adjusted.
side_effects: Drowsiness, weakness, dizziness.
age_limit: Adults and children above 12 years under supervision.

name: clarithromycin
when_to_use: Used to treat bacterial infections such as respiratory and skin infections.
how_to_use: Taken twice daily for 7–14 days as prescribed.
side_effects: Nausea, diarrhea, taste changes, heart rhythm issues (rare).
age_limit: Children and adults with prescription.
